title: Griselda Arroyo-Chávez
text:
Astrophysics • Star Formation • ISM


Griselda Arroyo-Chávez


Postdoctoral Research Associate · Steward Observatory · University of Arizona


I am a postdoctoral researcher in astrophysics working on star formation, the interstellar medium, filamentary structure, angular momentum transport, and synthetic observations. My work combines theory, numerical simulations, and comparisons with observations to study how structure and kinematics emerge across scales.


Explore Research Publications Contact

title: Griselda Arroyo-Chávez
text:
Academic Path
A brief view of my academic path and current research stage.






2013–2018



BSc in Physics


Universidad Veracruzana · Veracruz, Mexico


Supervisor: Dr. Alejandro Cruz Osorio


Simulations of neutron star collapse








2018–2024



MSc and PhD in Astrophysics


Institute of Radioastronomy and Astrophysics (IRyA), UNAM · Mexico


Supervisor: Prof. Enrique Vázquez-Semadeni


Evolution and redistribution of angular momentum in molecular clouds








2024–present



Postdoctoral Research Associate


Steward Observatory · University of Arizona


Supervisor: Dr. Shuo Kong


Angular momentum transport in interstellar filaments

title: Griselda Arroyo-Chávez
text:
Interactive Simulations


Filament Structure



This interactive rendering shows the filamentary gas structure in a magnetized, self-gravitating simulation. It highlights the spatial distribution of dense gas and the emergence of elongated structures across the computational domain.



Velocity Field



This visualization emphasizes the kinematic structure of the gas. It is intended to illustrate how large-scale flows, converging motions, and filamentary dynamics are organized in three dimensions.

title: Research
text: My work lies at the intersection of star formation theory, numerical simulations, and synthetic observations. I am broadly interested in how structure, kinematics, and environment shape the evolution of dense gas and the formation of stars.

title: Research
text: My work lies at the intersection of star formation theory, numerical simulations, and synthetic observations. I am broadly interested in how structure, kinematics, and environment shape the evolution of dense gas and the formation of stars.

title: Research
text:
Main themes

Filaments and cloud structure
Filaments are central to many models of cloud evolution and star formation. I study how they emerge, how they channel material, and how their internal velocity structure influences embedded collapse.


Angular momentum in collapsing systems
A major question in my work is how angular momentum is acquired, transported, and redistributed across scales. This includes the role of turbulent flows, geometry, accretion, and magnetic fields.


Numerical methods
I work with hydrodynamical and magnetohydrodynamical simulations, including particle-based and grid-based approaches, and use them to probe the physical origin of observable structure.


Synthetic observations
To compare theory with data, I generate synthetic continuum and line observations and analyze them in ways that parallel observational workflows.

title: Research
text:
Ongoing projects

Angular momentum versus filament orientation

Magnetic-field alignment in collapsing systems

Synthetic line cubes from numerical simulations

Filament identification and structural analysis

Comparison between numerical methods in star-formation problems
